elements.sent-2411-001: Note: Sub-titles are not captured in Xplore and should not be used Given Name Surname \IEEEauthorblockA{dept.}
elements.sent-2411-002: This document is a model and instructions for \LaTeX.
elements.sent-2411-003: This and the IEEEtran.cls file define the components of your paper [title, text, heads, etc.].
elements.sent-2411-004: Please observe the conference page limits.
elements.subsection-2411-001: Maintaining the Integrity of the Specifications
elements.sent-2411-005: The IEEEtran class file is used to format your paper and style the text.
elements.sent-2411-006: All margins, column widths, line spaces, and text fonts are prescribed; please do not alter them.
elements.sent-2411-008: For example, the head margin measures proportionately more than is customary.
elements.sent-2411-009: This measurement and others are deliberate, using specifications that anticipate your paper as one part of the entire proceedings, and not as an independent document.
elements.sent-2411-010: Please do not revise any of the current designations.
elements.sent-2411-011: Before you begin to format your paper, first write and save the content as a separate text file.
elements.sent-2411-012: Complete all content and organizational editing before formatting.
elements.sent-2411-013: Please note sections \ref{AA --\ref{SCM below for more information on proofreading, spelling and grammar.}}
elements.sent-2411-014: Keep your text and graphic files separate until after the text has been formatted and styled.
elements.sent-2411-015: Do not number text heads---{\LaTeX will do that for you.}
elements.sent-2411-016: Define abbreviations and acronyms the first time they are used in the text, even after they have been defined in the abstract.
elements.sent-2411-017: Abbreviations such as IEEE, SI, MKS, CGS, ac, dc, and rms do not have to be defined.
elements.sent-2411-018: Do not use abbreviations in the title or heads unless they are unavoidable.
elements.sent-2411-019: Use either SI (MKS) or CGS as primary units.
elements.sent-2411-020: An exception would be the use of English units as identifiers in trade, such as ``3.5-inch disk drive''.
elements.sent-2411-021: Avoid combining SI and CGS units, such as current in amperes and magnetic field in oersteds.
elements.sent-2411-022: This often leads to confusion because equations do not balance dimensionally.
elements.sent-2411-023: If you must use mixed units, clearly state the units for each quantity that you use in an equation.
elements.sent-2411-024: Do not mix complete spellings and abbreviations of units: ``Wb/m\textsuperscript{2 '' or ``webers per square meter'', not ``webers/m\textsuperscript{2 ''.}}
elements.sent-2411-025: Spell out units when they appear in text: ``.. .
elements.sent-2411-026: Use a zero before decimal points: ``0.25'', not ``.25''.
elements.sent-2411-029: To make your equations more compact, you may use the solidus (~/~), the exp function, or appropriate exponents.
elements.sent-2411-030: Italicize Roman symbols for quantities and variables, but not Greek symbols.
elements.sent-2411-031: Use a long dash rather than a hyphen for a minus sign.
elements.sent-2411-032: Punctuate equations with commas or periods when they are part of a sentence, as in: \begin{equation}a+b=\gamma\label{eq}\end{equation} Be sure that the symbols in your equation have been defined before or immediately following the equation.
elements.sent-2411-033: Use ``\eqref{eq '', not ``Eq.~\eqref{eq '' or ``equation \eqref{eq '', except at the beginning of a sentence: ``Equation \eqref{eq is .. .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-034: Please use ``soft'' (e.g., \verb|\eqref{Eq |) cross references instead of ``hard'' references (e.g., \verb|(1)|).}
elements.sent-2411-035: That will make it possible to combine sections, add equations, or change the order of figures or citations without having to go through the file line by line.
elements.sent-2411-036: Please don't use the \verb|{eqnarray | equation environment.}
elements.sent-2411-038: The \verb|{eqnarray | environment leaves unsightly spaces around relation symbols.}
elements.sent-2411-039: Please note that the \verb|{subequations | environment in {\LaTeX will increment the main equation counter even when there are no equation numbers displayed.}}
elements.sent-2411-040: If you forget that, you might write an article in which the equation numbers skip from (17) to (20), causing the copy editors to wonder if you've discovered a new method of counting.
elements.sent-2411-041: It doesn't get the bibliographic data from thin air but from .bib files.
elements.sent-2411-042: If you use {\BibTeX to produce a bibliography you must send the .bib files.}
elements.sent-2411-043: If you assign the same label to a subsubsection and a table, you might find that Table I has been cross referenced as Table IV-B3.
elements.sent-2411-044: If you put a \verb|\label| command before the command that updates the counter it's supposed to be using, the label will pick up the last counter to be cross referenced instead.
elements.sent-2411-045: In particular, a \verb|\label| command should not go before the caption of a figure or a table.
elements.sent-2411-046: Do not use \verb|\nonumber| inside the \verb|{array | environment.}
elements.sent-2411-047: It will not stop equation numbers inside \verb|{array | (there won't be any anyway) and it might stop a wanted equation number in the surrounding equation.}
elements.sent-2411-048: The word ``data'' is plural, not singular.
elements.sent-2411-049: The subscript for the permeability of vacuum $\mu_{0}$, and other common scientific constants, is zero with subscript formatting, not a lowercase letter ``o''.
elements.sent-2411-050: In American English, commas, semicolons, periods, question and exclamation marks are located within quotation marks only when a complete thought or name is cited, such as a title or full quotation.
elements.sent-2411-051: When quotation marks are used, instead of a bold or italic typeface, to highlight a word or phrase, punctuation should appear outside of the quotation marks.
elements.sent-2411-052: A parenthetical phrase or statement at the end of a sentence is punctuated outside of the closing parenthesis (like this).
elements.sent-2411-053: A graph within a graph is an ``inset'', not an ``insert''.
elements.sent-2411-054: The word alternatively is preferred to the word ``alternately'' (unless you really mean something that alternates).
elements.sent-2411-055: Do not use the word ``essentially'' to mean ``approximately'' or ``effectively''.
elements.sent-2411-056: In your paper title, if the words ``that uses'' can accurately replace the word ``using'', capitalize the ``u''; if not, keep using lower-cased.
elements.sent-2411-057: Be aware of the different meanings of the homophones ``affect'' and ``effect'', ``complement'' and ``compliment'', ``discreet'' and ``discrete'', ``principal'' and ``principle''.
elements.sent-2411-059: The prefix ``non'' is not a word; it should be joined to the word it modifies, usually without a hyphen.
elements.sent-2411-060: There is no period after the ``et'' in the Latin abbreviation ``et al.''.
elements.sent-2411-061: The abbreviation ``i.e.'' means ``that is'', and the abbreviation ``e.g.'' means ``for example''.
elements.sent-2411-062: An excellent style manual for science writers is \cite{b7 .}
elements.sent-2411-063: The class file is designed for, but not limited to, six authors.
elements.sent-2411-064: A minimum of one author is required for all conference articles.
elements.sent-2411-065: Author names should be listed starting from left to right and then moving down to the next line.
elements.sent-2411-066: This is the author sequence that will be used in future citations and by indexing services.
elements.sent-2411-067: Names should not be listed in columns nor group by affiliation.
elements.sent-2411-068: Please keep your affiliations as succinct as possible (for example, do not differentiate among departments of the same organization).
elements.sent-2411-069: Headings, or heads, are organizational devices that guide the reader through your paper.
elements.sent-2411-070: There are two types: component heads and text heads.
elements.sent-2411-071: Component heads identify the different components of your paper and are not topically subordinate to each other.
elements.sent-2411-072: Examples include Acknowledgments and References and, for these, the correct style to use is ``Heading 5''.
elements.sent-2411-073: Use ``figure caption'' for your Figure captions, and ``table head'' for your table title.
elements.sent-2411-074: Run-in heads, such as ``Abstract'', will require you to apply a style (in this case, italic) in addition to the style provided by the drop down menu to differentiate the head from the text.
elements.sent-2411-075: Text heads organize the topics on a relational, hierarchical basis.
elements.sent-2411-076: For example, the paper title is the primary text head because all subsequent material relates and elaborates on this one topic.
elements.sent-2411-077: If there are two or more sub-topics, the next level head (uppercase Roman numerals) should be used and, conversely, if there are not at least two sub-topics, then no subheads should be introduced.
elements.sent-2411-078: Place figures and tables at the top and bottom of columns.
elements.sent-2411-079: Avoid placing them in the middle of columns.
elements.sent-2411-080: Large figures and tables may span across both columns.
elements.sent-2411-081: Figure captions should be below the figures; table heads should appear above the tables.
elements.sent-2411-082: Insert figures and tables after they are cited in the text.
elements.sent-2411-083: Use the abbreviation ``Fig.~\ref{fig '', even at the beginning of a sentence.}
elements.figure-2411-001: \caption{Table Type Styles} \begin{center} \begin{tabular}{|c|c|c|c|} Table \multicolumn{3}{|c|}{Table Column Head} Head \textbf{Table column subhead} \textbf{Subhead} \textbf{Subhead} copy More table copy$^{\mathrm{a}}$ \multicolumn{4}{l}{$^{\mathrm{a}}$Sample of a Table footnote.} \end{tabular} \label{tab1} \end{center}
elements.sent-2411-084: Figure Labels: Use 8 point Times New Roman for Figure labels.
elements.sent-2411-085: Use words rather than symbols or abbreviations when writing Figure axis labels to avoid confusing the reader.
elements.sent-2411-086: As an example, write the quantity ``Magnetization'', or ``Magnetization, M'', not just ``M''.
elements.sent-2411-087: If including units in the label, present them within parentheses.
elements.sent-2411-088: Do not label axes only with units.
elements.sent-2411-089: In the example, write ``Magnetization (A/m)'' or ``Magnetization \{A[m(1)]\}'', not just ``A/m''.
elements.sent-2411-090: Do not label axes with a ratio of quantities and units.
elements.sent-2411-092: The preferred spelling of the word ``acknowledgment'' in America is without an ``e'' after the ``g''.
elements.sent-2411-093: Avoid the stilted expression ``one of us (R.. B.
elements.sent-2411-095: Put sponsor acknowledgments in the unnumbered footnote on the first page.
elements.sent-2411-096: The sentence punctuation follows the bracket \cite{b2 .}
elements.sent-2411-097: Refer simply to the reference number, as in \cite{b3 ---do not use ``Ref. \cite{b3 '' or ``reference \cite{b3 '' except at the beginning of a sentence: ``Reference \cite{b3 was the first $\ldots$'' Number footnotes separately in superscripts.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-098: Place the actual footnote at the bottom of the column in which it was cited.
elements.sent-2411-099: Do not put footnotes in the abstract or reference list.
elements.sent-2411-101: Unless there are six authors or more give all authors' names; do not use ``et al.''.
elements.sent-2411-102: Papers that have not been published, even if they have been submitted for publication, should be cited as ``unpublished'' \cite{b4 .}
elements.sent-2411-103: Papers that have been accepted for publication should be cited as ``in press'' \cite{b5 .}
elements.sent-2411-104: Capitalize only the first word in a paper title, except for proper nouns and element symbols.
elements.sent-2411-105: For papers published in translation journals, please give the English citation first, followed by the original foreign-language citation \cite{b6 .}
elements.sent-2411-106: Please ensure that all template text is removed from your conference paper prior to submission to the conference.
elements.sent-2411-107: Failure to remove the template text from your paper may result in your paper not being published.
elements.sent-2411-108: Our work aims to make significant strides in understanding unexplored locomotion control paradigms based on the integration of posture manipulation and thrust vectoring.
elements.sent-2411-109: These techniques are commonly seen in nature, such as Chukar birds using their wings to run on a nearly vertical wall.
elements.sent-2411-110: In this work, we show quadratic programming with contact constraints which is then given to the whole body controller to map on robot states to produce a thruster-assisted slope walking controller for our state-of-the-art Harpy platform.
elements.sent-2411-111: Harpy is a bipedal robot capable of legged-aerial locomotion using its legs and thrusters attached to its main frame.
elements.sent-2411-112: The optimization-based walking controller has been used for dynamic locomotion such as slope walking, but the addition of thrusters to perform inclined slope walking has not been extensively explored.
elements.sent-2411-113: In this work, we derive a thruster-assisted bipedal walking with the quadratic programming (QP) controller and implement it in simulation to study its performance.
elements.sent-2411-114: Legged robots offer a significant advantage over wheeled robots due to their ability to navigate complex and unstructured environments like forests, obstacles, and debris.
elements.sent-2411-115: However, controlling legged robots presents intricate challenges related to the hybrid nature of forces needed for movement, as the robot must establish and break contact with the ground using its feet.
elements.sent-2411-116: Initially, control design for legged robots relied on heuristic approaches, which achieved notable successes, such as Raibert’s \cite{raibert_dynamically_nodate walking controller, Jerry Pratt's \cite{pratt_capture_2006-1 capture point control, and Focchi's \cite{focchi_heuristic_2020 heuristic locomotion planning for quadrupedal robots.}}}
elements.sent-2411-117: Despite their success, heuristic methods have several limitations: 1) they are not easily generalizable to various terrains and motions; 2) they cannot predict the robot’s future state, thus failing to ensure the physical feasibility of planned trajectories.
elements.sent-2411-118: These limitations have driven research toward optimization-based predictive locomotion planning to avoid the short-sighted behaviors inherent in heuristic methods.
elements.sent-2411-119: There are many recent work that use optimization techniques to traverse inclined slopes \cite{ding_real-time_2019,kim_computationally-robust_2018,he_mechanism_2020 .}
elements.sent-2411-120: By framing locomotion planning as an optimization problem, high-level locomotion tasks can be represented as cost functions, and system dynamics can be incorporated as constraints.
elements.sent-2411-121: In addition to robot dynamics, locomotion tasks must also adhere to contact dynamics, such as unilateral force and friction cone constraints, which are crucial for stabilizing locomotion.
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[width=1\linewidth]{fig/Cover_image.png} \caption{Illustrates the Harpy platform, a bipedal robot with a thruster attached on each side which motivates our wing-assisted inclined walking.} \label{fig:cover-image}
elements.sent-2411-122: The primary contribution of this work is the development of a quadratic programming (QP) solver with contact and dynamic constraints.
elements.sent-2411-123: This combination is used to create a thruster-assisted walking controller on $30^{\circ}$ Inclined slope for our state-of-the-art Harpy platform, as illustrated in Fig.~\ref{fig:cover-image .}
elements.sent-2411-124: Recent tests have explored the application of thrusters (i.e., thrust vectoring) and posture manipulation in notable robots such as the Multi-modal mobility morphobot (M4) \cite{sihite_multi-modal_2023, sihite_efficient_2022, mandralis_minimum_2023 and LEONARDO \cite{kim_bipedal_2021, liang_rough-terrain_2021, sihite_optimization-free_2021, sihite_efficient_2022,pitroda_capture_2024-1 .}}
elements.sent-2411-125: The M4 robot aims to enhance locomotion versatility by combining posture manipulation and thrust vectoring, enabling modes such as walking, wheeling, flying, and loco-manipulation.
elements.sent-2411-126: LEONARDO, a quadcopter with two legs, can perform both quasi-static walking and flying.
elements.sent-2411-127: However, neither of these robots fully demonstrate dynamic legged locomotion and aerial mobility.
elements.sent-2411-128: Integrating these modes poses a significant challenge due to conflicting requirements (see \cite{sihite_multi-modal_2023 ).}
elements.sent-2411-129: These techniques are commonly used by birds which are known for their locomotion plasticity and robust locomotion feats.
elements.sent-2411-130: For instance, Chukar birds can perform a wing-assisted incline running (WAIR) maneuver \cite{dial_wing-assisted_2003-1, tobalske_aerodynamics_2007 .}
elements.sent-2411-131: In the WAIR maneuver, Chukar birds utilize their flapping wings and the resulting aerodynamic forces to increase contact forces, allowing them to ascend steep slopes that conventional bipedal robots would find challenging to navigate.
elements.sent-2411-132: In this study, we employ a detailed model of Harpy (depicted in Fig.~\ref{fig:cover-image ) using Matlab Simscape to evaluate our controller's effectiveness.}
elements.sent-2411-133: Harpy is equipped with eight custom-designed high-energy density actuators for dynamic walking, along with electric ducted fans mounted on its torso sides.
elements.sent-2411-134: Harpy's height measures 600 cm and weighs 6 Kg.
elements.sent-2411-135: It hosts a computer based on Elmo amplifiers for real-time low-level control command executions.
elements.sent-2411-136: Harpy's design integrates advantages from both aerial and dynamic bipedal-legged systems.
elements.sent-2411-137: Currently, the hardware design and assembly of Harpy have been completed~\cite{pitroda_dynamic_2023 , and our primary goal is to explore various control design strategies for this platform \cite{dangol_control_2021,pitroda_capture_2024,ramezani_performance_2014,Ramezani2013FeedbackCD .}}
elements.sent-2411-138: In this work, we aim to design a planning and control approach that enables stable walking on an inclined slope.
elements.sent-2411-139: The main contributions of this paper are: a) formulating a QP-based reference tracking controller which has dynamics of VLIP model.
elements.sent-2411-140: This work is structured as follows: we present the derivations of the Harpy high fidelity model, the reduced order model, followed by the Swing leg controller, Online planning using Quadratic Programming(QP), Whole body mapping, simulation results, and concluding remarks.
elements.figure-2411-004: \includegraphics[width=1\linewidth]{fig/Slop_walking_mdl.png} \caption{Illustrates Reduced-order model (ROM) and High fidelity model (HFM). ROM is represented as a point mass and massless link. HFM is represented as three inertia bodies which is used in numerical simulation.} \label{fig:rom-model}
elements.sent-2411-141: This section outlines the dynamics formulation of the robot which is used in the numerical simulation, in addition to the reduced order models which are used in the controller design.
elements.sent-2411-142: Figure \ref{fig:cover-image shows the kinematic configuration of Harpy which lists the center of mass (CoM) positions of the dynamic components, joint actuation torques, and thruster torques.}
elements.sent-2411-143: The system model has a combined total of 12 degrees-of-freedoms (DoFs): 6 for the body and 3 for each leg.
elements.sent-2411-144: Due to the symmetry, the left and right sides of the robot follow similar derivations so only the general derivations are provided in this section.
elements.sent-2411-145: The Harpy equations of motion are derived using the Euler-Lagrangian dynamics formulation.
elements.sent-2411-146: In order to simplify the system, each link is assumed to be mass-less, with the mass and inertia concentrated at the body and each motor.
elements.sent-2411-147: Consequently, the lower leg kinematic chain is considered massless, significantly simplifying the system.
elements.sent-2411-148: The three leg joints are labeled as the hip frontal (pelvis $P$), hip sagittal (hip $H$), and knee sagittal (knee $K$), as illustrated in Fig. \ref{fig:cover-image .}
elements.sent-2411-149: The thrusters are also considered massless and capable of providing forces in any direction to simplify the problem.
elements.sent-2411-150: Let $\gamma_h$ be the frontal hip angle, while $\phi_h$ and $\phi_k$ represent the sagittal hip and knee angles, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-151: The superscripts $\{B,P,H,K\}$ represent the frame of reference about the body, pelvis, hip, and knee, while the inertial frame is represented without the superscript.
elements.sent-2411-152: Let $R_B$ be the rotation matrix from the body frame to the inertial frame (i.e., $\bm x = R_B\, \bm x^B$).
elements.sent-2411-153: The pelvis motor mass is added to the body mass.
elements.sent-2411-154: Then, the positions of the hip and knee centers of mass (CoM) are defined using kinematic equations: \begin{equation}\begin{gathered} \bm{p}_P = \bm{p}_{B} + R_{B}\, \bm{l}_{1}^{B}, \bm{p}_H = \bm{p}_{P} + R_{B}\,R_x(\gamma_h)\, \bm{l}_{2}^{P} \bm{p}_K = \bm{p}_{H} + R_{B}\,R_x(\gamma_h)\,R_y(\phi_h) \bm{l}_{3}^{H}, \end{gathered} \label{eq:pos_com}\end{equation} where $R_x$ and $R_y$ are the rotation matrices about the $x$ and $y$ axes, respectively, and $\bm l$ is the length vector representing the configuration of Harpy, which remains constant in its respective local frame of reference.
elements.formula-2411-002: \begin{equation}\begin{gathered} p_P = p_{B} + R_{B}\, l_{1}^{B}, p_H = p_{P} + R_{B}\,R_x(\gamma_h)\, l_{2}^{P} p_K = p_{H} + R_{B}\,R_x(\gamma_h)\,R_y(\phi_h) l_{3}^{H}, \end{gathered} \label{eq:pos_com}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-155: The positions of the foot and thrusters are defined as: \begin{equation}\begin{gathered} \bm{p}_F = \bm{p}_{K} + R_{B}\,R_x(\gamma_h)\,R_y(\phi_h)\,R_y(\phi_k)\, \bm{l}_{4}^{K} \bm{p}_T = \bm{p}_{B} + R_{B}\, \bm{l}_{t}^{B} \end{gathered} \label{eq:pos_other}\end{equation} where the length vector from the knee to the foot is $\bm l_4^K = [-l_{4a}\cos{\phi_k , 0, -( l_{4b + l_{4a}\sin{\phi_k )]^\top$, which represents the kinematic solution to the parallel linkage mechanism of the lower leg.}}}
elements.formula-2411-003: \begin{equation}\begin{gathered} p_F = p_{K} + R_{B}\,R_x(\gamma_h)\,R_y(\phi_h)\,R_y(\phi_k)\, l_{4}^{K} p_T = p_{B} + R_{B}\, l_{t}^{B} \end{gathered} \label{eq:pos_other}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-156: Let $\bm \omega_B$ be the angular velocity of the body.
elements.sent-2411-157: Then, the angular velocities of the hip and knee are defined as: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \bm \omega_H^B = [\dot{\gamma}_h,0,0]^\top + \bm \omega_B^B \bm \omega_K^H = [0,\dot{\phi}_h,0]^\top + \bm \omega_H^H \end{aligned}\end{equation} Consequently, the total energy of Harpy for the Lagrangian dynamics formulation is defined as follows: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} K = \tfrac{1}{2} \textstyle \sum_{i \in \mathcal{F}} \left( m_i\,\bm p_i^\top\, \bm p_i + \bm \omega_i^{i \top} \, \hat I_i \, \bm \omega_i^i \right) V = - \textstyle \sum_{i \in \mathcal{F}} \left( m_i\,\bm p_i^\top\, [0,0,-g]^\top \right), \end{aligned} \label{eq:energy}\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-005: \begin{equation} K = \tfrac{1}{2} \textstyle \sum_{i \in F} \left( m_i\,\bm p_i^\top\, \bm p_i + \bm \omega_i^{i \top} \, \hat I_i \, \bm \omega_i^i \right) V = - \textstyle \sum_{i \in F} \left( m_i\,\bm p_i^\top\, [0,0,-g]^\top \right),  \label{eq:energy}\end{equation}
elements.figure-2411-005: \includegraphics[width=0.9\linewidth]{fig/Slope_walking.png} \caption{Snapshot of the simulation result, showing thruster-assisted inclined slope walking using the QP controller.} \label{fig:snapshot}
elements.sent-2411-158: In this context, $\mathcal{F = \{B,H_L,K_L,H_R,K_R\}$ denotes the relevant frames of reference and mass components, including the body, left hip, left knee, right hip, right knee.}
elements.sent-2411-159: The subscripts L and R indicate the left and right sides of the robot, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-160: Additionally, $\hat I_i$ represents the inertia relative to its local frame, and $g$ is the gravitational constant.
elements.sent-2411-161: This forms the Lagrangian of the system, expressed as $L = K - V$, which is used to derive the Euler-Lagrange equations of motion.
elements.sent-2411-162: The body's angular velocity dynamics are obtained using a modified Lagrangian for rotation in $SO(3)$, avoiding the use of Euler angles and the associated risk of gimbal lock.
elements.sent-2411-163: Consequently, the following equations of motion are based on Hamilton's principle of least action: \begin{equation}\begin{gathered} \tfrac{d}{dt}\left( \tfrac{\partial L}{\partial \bm \omega_B^B} \right) + \bm \omega_B^B \times \tfrac{\partial L}{\partial \bm \omega_B^B} + \textstyle \sum_{j=1}^{3} \bm r_{Bj} \times \tfrac{\partial L}{\partial \bm r_{Bj}} = \bm u_1, \tfrac{d}{dt}\left( \tfrac{\partial L}{\partial \dot {\bm q}} \right) - \tfrac{\partial L}{\partial \bm q} = \bm u_2, \tfrac{d}{dt} R_B = R_B\, [\bm \omega_B^B]_\times, \end{gathered} \label{eq:eom_eulerlagrange}\end{equation} where $[\, \cdot \, ]_\times$ denotes the skew symmetric matrix, $R_B^\top = [\bm r_{B1 , \bm r_{B2 , \bm r_{B3 ]$, $\bm q = [\bm p_B^\top, \gamma_{h_L , \gamma_{h_R , \phi_{h_L , \phi_{h_R ]^\top$ represents the dynamical system states other than $(R_B,\bm \omega^B_B)$, and $\bm u$ denotes the generalized forces.}}}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-006: \begin{equation}\begin{gathered} \tfrac{d}{dt}\left( \tfrac{\partial L}{\partial \bm \omega_B^B} \right) + \bm \omega_B^B \times \tfrac{\partial L}{\partial \bm \omega_B^B} + \textstyle \sum_{j=1}^{3} \bm r_{Bj} \times \tfrac{\partial L}{\partial \bm r_{Bj}} = \bm u_1, \tfrac{d}{dt}\left( \tfrac{\partial L}{\partial \dot {\bm q}} \right) - \tfrac{\partial L}{\partial \bm q} = \bm u_2, \tfrac{d}{dt} R_B = R_B\, [\bm \omega_B^B]_\times, \end{gathered} \label{eq:eom_eulerlagrange}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-164: There is no mass associated with the knee sagittal angle $\phi_k$, and thus, it is updated using the joint acceleration input $\bm u_k = [\ddot{\phi _{k_L , \ddot{\phi _{k_R ]^\top$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-165: Then, the system acceleration can be derived as follows: \begin{equation}\begin{gathered} M \bm a + \bm h = B_j\, \bm u_j + \bm u_t + B_g\, \bm u_g \end{gathered} \label{eq:eom_accel}\end{equation} where $\bm a = [ \dot{\bm \omega _B^{B\top , \ddot{\bm q ^\top, \ddot{\phi _{k_L , \ddot{\phi _{k_R ]^\top$, $\bm u_t$ denotes the generalized thruster force, $\bm u_j = [u_{P_L , u_{P_R , u_{H_L , u_{H_R , \bm u_k^\top]^\top$ represents the joint actuation, and $\bm u_g$ stands for the ground reaction forces (GRFs).}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-007: \begin{equation}\begin{gathered} M \bm a + \bm h = B_j\, \bm u_j + \bm u_t + B_g\, \bm u_g \end{gathered} \label{eq:eom_accel}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-166: The variables $M$, $\bm h$ and $B_g$ are functions of the full system states: \begin{equation}\bm x = [\bm r_{B}^\top, \bm q^\top, \phi_{K_L}, \phi_{K_R}, \bm \omega_B^{B \top}, \dot{\bm q}^\top, \dot{\phi}_{K_L}, \dot{\phi}_{K_R}]^\top, \label{eq:states}\end{equation} where the vector $\bm r_B$ contains the elements of $R_B$.
elements.sent-2411-167: Introducing $B_j = [0_{6 \times 6 , I_{6 \times 6 ]$ allows $\bm u_j$ to actuate the joint angles directly.}}
elements.sent-2411-168: Let $\bm v = [\bm \omega_B^{B\top , \dot{\bm q ^\top]^\top$ denote the velocity of the generalized coordinates.}}
elements.sent-2411-169: Then, $B_t$ and $B_g$ can be defined using the virtual displacement from the velocity as follows: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} B_g = \begin{bmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} \partial \dot{\bm p}_{F_L} / \partial \bm v \partial \dot{\bm p}_{F_R} / \partial \bm v \end{pmatrix}^\top 0_{2 \times 6} \end{bmatrix}. \end{aligned} \label{eq:generalized_forces}\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-170: The GRF is modeled using the unilateral compliant ground model with undamped rebound, while friction is modeled using the Stribeck friction model, defined as follows: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} u_{g,z} = -k_{g,p}\, p_{F,z} - k_{g,d}\, \dot p_{F,z} u_{g,x} = -\left(\mu_c + (\mu_s - \mu_c)\, \mathrm{exp}\left(-\tfrac{|\dot p_{F,x}|^2}{v_s^2}\right) \right) f_z\, \mathrm{sgn}(\dot p_{F,x}) - \mu_v\,\dot p_{F,x}, \end{aligned} \label{eq:ground_model}\end{equation} where $p_{F,x}$ and $p_{F,z}$ represent the $x$ and $z$ components of the inertial foot position, $k_{g,p}$ and $k_{g,d}$ denote the spring and damping model for the ground, $\mu_c$, $\mu_s$, and $\mu_v$ are the Coulomb, static, and viscous friction coefficients, respectively, and $v_s$ is the Stribeck velocity.
elements.formula-2411-010: \begin{equation} u_{g,z} = -k_{g,p}\, p_{F,z} - k_{g,d}\, \dot p_{F,z} u_{g,x} = -\left(\mu_c + (\mu_s - \mu_c)\, exp\left(-\tfrac{|\dot p_{F,x}|^2}{v_s^2}\right) \right) f_z\, sgn(\dot p_{F,x}) - \mu_v\,\dot p_{F,x},  \label{eq:ground_model}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-171: Then, the ground force model $\bm u_g$ is defined as follows: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \bm u_g = [\bm u_{g_L}^\top\, H(-p_{F_L,z}),\, \bm u_{g_R}^\top\, H(-p_{F_R,z})]^\top, \end{aligned} \label{eq:ground_forces}\end{equation} where $H(x)$ denotes the Heaviside function, while $\bm u_{g_L}$ and $\bm u_{g_R}$ represent the left and right ground forces, which are formed using their respective components $u_{g,x}$, $u_{g,y}$, and $u_{g,z}$.
elements.sent-2411-172: The full-dynamics model can be derived using equations \eqref{eq:eom_eulerlagrange to \eqref{eq:ground_forces to form \begin{equation}\dot{\bm x} = \bm f(\bm x, \bm u_j, \bm u_t, \bm u_g).\end{equation}.}}
elements.formula-2411-012: \begin{equation}\dot{\bm x} = \bm f(\bm x, \bm u_j, \bm u_t, \bm u_g).\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-173: In the VLIP model, the center of pressure (CoP), denoted as $\bm c$, is defined as the weighted average position of the feet, given by $\bm c = \lambda_L\, \bm p_{F_L + \lambda_R\, \bm p_{F_R}$, where $\lambda_i = u_{g_i,z / (u_{g_L,z + u_{g_R,z )$ for $i \in \{L,R\}$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-174: In the Harpy full-dynamics model, which uses a point foot, $\bm c$ equals the stance foot position during the SS phase.
elements.sent-2411-175: The VLIP model without thrusters is underactuated, but the addition of thrusters makes the system fully actuated and enables trajectory tracking.
elements.sent-2411-176: For using the VLIP model in QP controller, we project the VLIP model on the sagittal plane.
elements.sent-2411-177: Equation of motion are derived assuming no slip $\left( \ddot{ \bm c = 0 \right)$ as follows: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} m \ddot{p}_{B,x} = - mgsin\left(\alpha \right) + u_{t,x} -\lambda_{x} m \ddot{p}_{B,z} = - mgcosQP \left(\alpha \right) + u_{t,z} + \lambda_{z} \end{aligned} \label{eq:model_vlip}\end{equation} where $m$ represents the mass of the VLIP model, which in this case is the total mass of the system, and $ [u_{t,x ,u_{t,z ]$ denotes the thruster forces about the CoM.}}}
elements.formula-2411-013: \begin{equation} m \ddot{p}_{B,x} = - mgsin\left(\alpha \right) + u_{t,x} -\lambda_{x} m \ddot{p}_{B,z} = - mgcosQP \left(\alpha \right) + u_{t,z} + \lambda_{z}  \label{eq:model_vlip}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-178: The constraint force $[\lambda_{x ,\lambda_{z ]$ is established to maintain the leg length $r$ equal to the leg conformation.}}
elements.sent-2411-179: By solving the zero moment point (ZMP) about the center of motion would result in the following equation. \begin{equation}\begin{gathered} \left( m \ddot{P}_{B,x} + \lambda_{x} \right)P_{B,z} = \left( m \ddot{P}_{B,z} - \lambda_{z} \right) \left(c_{x} - P_{B,x} \right) \end{gathered} \label{eq:model_vlip_ZMP}\end{equation} The linear pendulum model can be enforced by setting $P_{B,z = z_0$ and $\ddot{P _{B,z = 0$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-180: By setting LIP constraints, equation \eqref{eq:model_vlip_ZMP will be become \begin{equation}\begin{gathered} \ddot{P}_{B,x} = \frac{\left(- \lambda_{z} \right) \left(c_{x} - P_{B,x} \right)}{m z_{0}} - \frac{\lambda_{x}}{m} \end{gathered} \label{eq:model_vlip_acc}\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-181: The swing leg joint controller is designed to follow the desired foot position.
elements.sent-2411-182: The reference trajectory is generated by one dimensional fourth-order bezier polynomial. \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} b_i(s) = \sum_{k=0}^{M} \beta_{ik} \frac{M!}{k!(M-k)!} s^k (1-s)^{M-k} \end{aligned} \label{eq:bezier_traj}\end{equation} where $s$ is the normalized gait timing variable varying from $0$ to $1$.
elements.sent-2411-183: The initial and final velocity of the gait is set to zero by setting $\beta_{1 = \beta{0}$ and $\beta^{i _{M = \beta^{i _{M-1}$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-184: This is done to avoid a large impact.
elements.sent-2411-185: Target joint angles $\bm q_t$ were found using inverse kinematics.
elements.sent-2411-186: Let $q = [\gamma_H, \phi_H, \phi_K]^{\top}$ represent the joint angles of the legs.
elements.sent-2411-187: Given the trajectory $\bm q_t$, the swing joint controller $\bm u_{sw}$ can be derived using a simple PID controller. \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \bm u_{sw} = K_p \bm e + K_i \int_{0}^{t} \bm e(\tau) d\tau + K_d \frac{\partial \bm e(t)}{\partial t} \end{aligned} \label{eq:swing torque}\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-017: \begin{equation} \bm u_{sw} = K_p \bm e + K_i \int_{0}^{t} \bm e(\tau) d\tau + K_d \frac{\partial \bm e(t)}{\partial t}  \label{eq:swing torque}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-188: The objective of the stance controller is to maintain friction cone constraints and propel the body to track reference position and velocity.
elements.sent-2411-189: We proceed with eliminating the states from the decision variables by expressing them as a function of current state $x_{0}$ and control efforts \begin{equation}\bm x = F\bm x_{0} + G\bm u\end{equation} where \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \bm x = \left[x^\top_{0}, x^\top_{1}, x^\top_{2}, \hdots x^\top_{N}\right]^\top \bm u = \left[u^\top_{0}, u^\top_{1}, u^\top_{2}, \hdots u^\top_{N-1} \right]^\top \end{aligned}\end{equation} \begin{equation}F =  I A_d \vdots A^{N}_d  G =  0 B_d 0 \vdots \ddots A^{N-1}_dB_d A^{N-2}_dB_d \hdots A_d B_d B_d \end{equation} QP cost is tracking error with decision variable as $\bm u$. \begin{equation}\min_{\bm u}~\bm u^\top P\bm u + c^\top \bm u \label{eq:qp-formulation}\end{equation} subject to \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} A_{in}\bm u < B_{in} \end{aligned} \label{eq:qp-constraints}\end{equation} \begin{equation}A_{in} =  I -I \vdots \ddots I 0 \hdots -I  B_{in} =  \bm u_{u,0} -\bm u_{l,0} \vdots \bm u_{u,n} -\bm u_{l,n} \end{equation} Here, $\bm u_{u}$ and $\bm u_{l}$ are the bounds from the friction cone.
elements.formula-2411-019: \begin{equation} \bm x = \left[x^\top_{0}, x^\top_{1}, x^\top_{2}, \hdots x^\top_{N}\right]^\top \bm u = \left[u^\top_{0}, u^\top_{1}, u^\top_{2}, \hdots u^\top_{N-1} \right]^\top \end{equation}
elements.formula-2411-020: \begin{equation}F =  I A_d \vdots A^{N}_d  G =  0 B_d 0 \vdots \ddots A^{N-1}_dB_d A^{N-2}_dB_d \hdots A_d B_d B_d \end{equation}
elements.formula-2411-021: \begin{equation}\min_{\bm u}~\bm u^\top P\bm u + c^\top \bm u \label{eq:qp-formulation}\end{equation}
elements.formula-2411-023: \begin{equation}A_{in} =  I -I \vdots \ddots I 0 \hdots -I  B_{in} =  \bm u_{u,0} -\bm u_{l,0} \vdots \bm u_{u,n} -\bm u_{l,n} \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-191: We have constrained QP with inequality constraints and dense matrices, where $P$ and $c$ are defined as follows: \begin{equation}P = G^\top QG + R c = \left(\bm x_{0}- \bm x_{d,0}\right) F^\top QG \label{eq:qp-matrices}\end{equation} In this case, $\bm x_{0 $ are the initial states and $\bm x_{d}$ are the reference for those states.}
elements.formula-2411-024: \begin{equation}P = G^\top QG + R c = \left(\bm x_{0}- \bm x_{d,0}\right) F^\top QG \label{eq:qp-matrices}\end{equation}

elements.sent-2411-192: In previous section, QP solver outputs ground reaction forces $\left[\lambda_{x ,\lambda_{z}\right]^\top$.}
elements.sent-2411-193: Given ground reaction force and acceleration of the body, we used equation \eqref{eq:model_vlip to compute the generalized thruster force $\bm u_{t = [u_{t,x ,0,u_{t,z ]$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-194: To find the stance leg joint torque, we create a mapping matrix.
elements.sent-2411-195: The states of the robot remain the same as that of the formulation shown in the~\eqref{eq:states .}
elements.sent-2411-196: The equation of motion is derived using Euler Lagrangian dynamics formulation. \begin{equation}\begin{gathered} M \ddot{\bm q} + \bm h = B_j\, \bm u_j + \bm u_t + B_g\, \bm u_g \end{gathered} \label{eq:Mapping_eom}\end{equation} In this case, stance leg $\bm u_{g}$ is given by the Qp solver and swing leg $\bm u_g$ is equal to zero.
elements.formula-2411-025: \begin{equation}\begin{gathered} M \ddot{\bm q} + \bm h = B_j\, \bm u_j + \bm u_t + B_g\, \bm u_g \end{gathered} \label{eq:Mapping_eom}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-197: Given that the QP solver ensures adherence to the friction cone condition, it is feasible to implement stance contact force constraints. \begin{equation}\begin{gathered} J_s \ddot{\bm q} = - \dot{J}_s \dot{\bm q} \end{gathered} \label{eq:contact_constraint}\end{equation} where $J_s = [\partial \dot{\bm P _F / \partial \dot{\bm q ]^{\top}$ is the contact jacobian.}}
elements.sent-2411-198: Further, we use $2$D reduced-order model and thus we implement planner constraint. \begin{equation}\begin{gathered} J_c \ddot{\bm q} = [0,0,0]^{\top} \end{gathered} \label{eq:planner constraint}\end{equation} here, $J_c$ is constraint jacobian and it ensures $y$, roll and yaw acceleration are set to zero.
elements.sent-2411-199: The numerical simulation (depicted in Fig. \ref{fig:snapshot ) and optimization are performed on Matlab where we use RK4 scheme to propagate the model forward in time.}
elements.sent-2411-200: The Condensed Quadratic Programming (QP) formulation \eqref{eq:qp-formulation , utilizes the qpSWIFT package \cite{pandala_qpswift_2019 .}}
elements.sent-2411-201: The Qp is running at $100$ Hz and numerical simulation is running at $2000$ Hz.
elements.sent-2411-202: In this section, all units are in N, kg, m, s.

elements.sent-2411-206: For whole body controller, we only consider mass and body inertia.
elements.sent-2411-208: The weighting matrices for QP were $Q = diag([300000, 2000])$ and $R = diag([1, 1])$.

elements.sent-2411-217: In this paper, we propose a QP formulation that is computationally efficient, and algorithmically robust.
elements.sent-2411-218: The proposed algorithm ensures internal and contact constraints are satisfied.
elements.sent-2411-219: It uses QP and a reduced-order model to compute reaction forces satisfying contact constraints and then provides the thruster forces.
elements.sent-2411-220: The whole body controller then maps these forces on a high-fidelity model to produce thruster-assisted slope walking.
elements.sent-2411-221: In our numerical simulation tests, QP computes contact force forces for a robot in 0.3ms.
elements.sent-2411-222: The swing leg controller provides the required torque to track the generated open-loop trajectory and complete the gait cycles.
elements.sent-2411-223: The QP solver was quickly able to achieve a stable limit cycle showing the efficiency of the control method.
elements.sent-2411-224: Future work will focus on extending the method to 3D motion.
elements.sent-2411-225: Also, the current formulation uses a PID controller for the swing leg, which can be replaced by a heuristic method such as Raibert's control or capture point control.
